_source: 每種能量結構被利用的模式辨識，依面向（personal/seeker/company）分別定義

personal.healthy: They give you opportunities and resources, and you grow while staying grateful
personal.red_flag: They decide things for you under the banner of 'helping,' and get angry when you push back
personal.test: Help you asked for = healthy. Help you didn't ask for that they insist is 'for your own good' = watch out
personal.action: Say it straight: 'I didn't ask for this.' If they can't accept your no, that's not help -- that's control

seeker.healthy: The company gives you real promotion opportunities and resources, and you create value in return
seeker.red_flag: They use 'we're investing in you' as an excuse to underpay, and promotions are always empty promises
seeker.test: Promotions and raises have a concrete timeline = healthy. Always 'just wait a bit longer' = red flag
seeker.action: Ask for specific promotion criteria and timelines. A promise with no details isn't a promise

company.healthy: {name} lifts the team and actively pursues opportunities
company.red_flag: {name} only wants to be lifted without contributing to the team
company.test: Elevates the whole team = healthy. Only climbs for themselves = red flag
company.action: Watch whether they push the team up or step on the team to climb. Ask how they've helped colleagues

ex.healthy: You walked away with real growth -- their help genuinely made you better
ex.red_flag: After it ended, you realized their 'help' was actually control. You weren't being elevated, you were being led around
ex.test: You feel freer after leaving = that help was control. You're genuinely stronger = the help was real
ex.action: Save this insight for the next one. When someone wants to 'help' you, ask yourself: is this the direction I want?

personal.healthy: You naturally take care of them, and they appreciate it and give back
personal.red_flag: They take endlessly, never reciprocate, and treat your effort as a given. Stop giving and they get moody
personal.test: Your giving is appreciated = healthy. They only think of you when they need something = red flag
personal.action: Stop giving for a week and watch. A grateful person asks what's wrong. An exploiter gets angry or vanishes

seeker.healthy: The company values your steady commitment and gives you training and growth resources
seeker.red_flag: They're nice to you when they're short-staffed, but once you're in, no training and no resources
seeker.test: There's a concrete training plan after joining = healthy. You're left to figure things out alone = red flag
seeker.action: Ask about the specifics of the training program in the interview. Vague answers mean the company doesn't deserve your loyalty

company.healthy: {name} is committed and willing to grow with the company long-term
company.red_flag: {name} talks about long-term commitment but is always ready to jump ship
company.test: Past jobs lasted two years or more = worth considering. Switches every six months = be careful
company.action: Ask why they left their last few jobs. If it's always someone else's fault, that's a sign

ex.healthy: You know your giving meant something -- the other person became better because of you
ex.red_flag: After it ended, you realized you were the only one giving. They left and never looked back
ex.test: They still thank you after it ended = your giving was valued. They disappeared = you were used up
ex.action: Set a checkpoint in the next relationship: after three months, is your giving being reciprocated? If not, cut your losses early

personal.healthy: You actively maintain the relationship, and they value it and reciprocate
personal.red_flag: They only show up when they need a favor and disappear otherwise. You're not a friend, you're a free resource
personal.test: Two-way contact = healthy. You're always the one reaching out, checking in, helping = time to rethink
personal.action: Stop reaching out and see if they come to you. Someone who never shows up unless you initiate -- you know what that means

seeker.healthy: Your networking and collaboration skills are put to good use, and you have a platform to shine
seeker.red_flag: The company dumps all cross-department coordination on you, but your actual work goes unrecognized
seeker.test: Your social investment counts toward your performance = healthy. All effort, no credit = red flag
seeker.action: Clarify your responsibilities. Cross-department coordination is an extra contribution, not an obligation
seeker.drain_warning: You're the giver. The other side is comfortable while you drain. Watch whether you're the only one contributing.

company.healthy: {name} brings networking value and integrates into the team quickly
company.red_flag: {name} is great socially but lacks professional skills -- all relationships, no substance
company.test: Social skills translate into work results = healthy. Social but doesn't deliver = red flag
company.action: Besides communication skills, look for concrete professional output in the interview

ex.healthy: This relationship taught you how to actively build connections
ex.red_flag: After it ended, you realized you were the only one keeping it alive
ex.test: They reached out after the split = you weren't the only one investing. They vanished = you were free service
ex.action: In the next relationship, watch: if you stop initiating, does the other person step up? The answer will save you a lot of time

personal.healthy: They take care of you, and you accept help while keeping your own judgment
personal.red_flag: Their caretaking breeds dependency. Without them, you feel like you can't do anything
personal.test: You can still act independently after receiving help = healthy. You fall apart without them = red flag
personal.action: Deliberately do something you thought only they could handle. If you pull it off, you'll know it was habit, not inability

seeker.healthy: The company has real problems for you to solve, and solving them gives you a sense of achievement
seeker.red_flag: Problems never end because the company itself is a problem factory
seeker.test: Problems are trending down = healthy. You fix one and the company breaks two more = red flag
seeker.action: Watch for three months: are the problems you solve being prevented systemically? If not, it's a bottomless pit
seeker.drain_warning: You receive energy but it decays over time. Year one is the best; the drain becomes obvious in bad fortune years.

company.healthy: {name} is good at solving problems and steadily removes obstacles
company.red_flag: {name} only fixes problems but never prevents them -- always firefighting
company.test: Problems are being systemically prevented = healthy. Same problems keep recurring = red flag
company.action: Ask how they prevent problems from happening again. Someone who can only fight fires but can't build firewalls has limited value

ex.healthy: They took care of you while you maintained your independence
ex.red_flag: After it ended, you realized you can't do anything on your own because they handled everything
ex.test: You can live independently after the split = you weren't dependent. You fell apart = you were cared for until you lost the ability to stand alone
ex.action: Start practicing independence now. Not for getting back together -- for yourself

personal.healthy: You build the environment, and they grow steadily within it
personal.red_flag: They keep destroying what you build. You create stability, they tear it down, and you start over
personal.test: You build and they maintain = healthy. You build, they wreck, you rebuild = you're feeding a black hole
personal.action: Stop rebuilding what gets destroyed. If they collapse because of it, that's their problem, not yours

seeker.healthy: The company needs you to build systems and processes, and your groundwork is recognized
seeker.red_flag: Everything you build keeps getting scrapped and redone -- nobody values it
seeker.test: Your systems are adopted and maintained = healthy. Built then torn down repeatedly = red flag
seeker.action: Confirm who has decision-making power and maintenance responsibility. Systems nobody maintains aren't worth building
seeker.drain_warning: You lead but must constantly output energy to maintain stability. Long-term fatigue accumulates quietly.

company.healthy: {name} builds systems and processes -- stable and reliable
company.red_flag: {name} is overly cautious, wants to systematize everything but moves too slowly
company.test: System-building delivers real results = healthy. Building systems for the sake of it = red flag
company.action: Ask what concrete improvements their past systems brought. If they can't give numbers, think twice

ex.healthy: What you built for them helped them grow, and that value doesn't disappear just because you split up
ex.red_flag: You kept building, they kept destroying. After the split, you realized it was all wasted effort
ex.test: You feel relieved after the split = you were drained. You feel it's a shame = the building had real value
ex.action: Remember for the next one: what you build only matters if someone maintains it. People who won't maintain aren't worth building for

personal.healthy: They give to you, and you're grateful and do your best to give back
personal.red_flag: They guilt-trip you with 'look how much I've done for you.' You try to make your own choices and they pull the sacrifice card
personal.test: Their giving is voluntary with no strings attached = healthy. Giving becomes leverage = red flag
personal.action: Gratitude is gratitude, but your decisions can't be held hostage by anyone's sacrifices. 'Thank you, but this one is my call'

seeker.healthy: The company has tough problems for you to tackle, and your crisis management skills are valued
seeker.red_flag: You're only ever cleaning up messes -- the good stuff never comes your way
seeker.test: Positive feedback after handling a crisis = healthy. Nobody remembers after it's done = red flag
seeker.action: Make sure your crisis work is documented and recognized. Sacrifice without feedback isn't worth it
seeker.drain_warning: Classical warning: 'beyond endurance.' Your energy is continuously extracted. Without strong external support, expect difficulty after 2-3 years.

company.healthy: {name} handles pressure well and has a solid approach to tough problems
company.red_flag: {name} only performs under pressure and creates conflict when things are calm
company.test: Returns to normal after a crisis = healthy. Can't sit still without one and creates their own = red flag
company.action: Watch their behavior in a low-pressure interview. If they can't stay calm, they can fight battles but can't govern in peace

ex.healthy: You're grateful for what they gave, and there were good times even if the relationship was uneven
ex.red_flag: After the split, they won't let go and keep bringing up how much they did for you
ex.test: They let go after the split = the giving was genuine. They won't let go = the giving was a tool for control
ex.action: Gratitude is gratitude. A breakup is a breakup. Using past sacrifices to chain your future -- that's a red flag

personal.healthy: Early friction is tough, but you can see improvement over time
personal.red_flag: It's always rough and the good part never comes. They use 'we just need time' to make you wait forever
personal.test: Small improvements in three months = normal. A year in with zero change = it's not rough-then-smooth, it's just rough
personal.action: Set a clear deadline, then evaluate. No progress means it's not a timing issue -- it's a people issue

seeker.healthy: The company has a healthy social culture, and your coordination skills have room to shine
seeker.red_flag: The company looks friendly on the surface but is cliquish underneath -- social events become political
seeker.test: Social investment leads to real collaboration = healthy. Just surface schmoozing = red flag
seeker.action: Watch whether social interactions translate into actual teamwork. Pure schmoozing is wasting your energy
seeker.drain_warning: Pressure-driven growth — strong short-term but the drain is real. In bad fortune years, pressure may crush rather than push.

company.healthy: {name} has strong social skills and can boost team morale
company.red_flag: {name}'s socializing turns into office politics and cliques
company.test: Socializing leads to positive collaboration = healthy. Socializing becomes picking sides and exclusion = red flag
company.action: Ask former colleagues for their take. Polarized opinions are a warning sign

ex.healthy: After the rough patch, there really were good times. The rough-then-smooth rhythm was real
ex.red_flag: It was always rough. The smooth part never came. You just kept enduring
ex.test: Looking back, was there ever a genuinely comfortable phase? Yes = the rhythm was real. No = you were strung along
ex.action: In the next relationship, set a checkpoint: if nothing improves in three months, it's not a rhythm issue -- it's a people issue

personal.healthy: You learn and grow together, and both sides make progress
personal.red_flag: You're the only one doing the work while they enjoy the results. Or they weaponize your drive: 'You're so driven, do this for me'
personal.test: Both sides are progressing = healthy. Only you're working while they watch = you're not a partner, you're free labor
personal.action: Next time they ask for help, ask 'How are you going to contribute?' Their reaction tells you everything

seeker.healthy: The company gives you learning opportunities, and your project results are recognized
seeker.red_flag: The company pushes you to keep learning new things with no time to absorb, or your boss takes credit for your work
seeker.test: What you learn gets put into practice = healthy. You learn but can't apply it, or credit gets stolen = red flag
seeker.action: Confirm who owns the project results. If your work always has someone else's name on it, it's time to go

company.healthy: {name} learns fast and delivers well on projects
company.red_flag: {name} only wants to learn, not to deliver -- treating the company as a free school
company.test: Learning leads to output = healthy. Learns and leaves = red flag
company.action: Check if they actually applied what they learned in past roles. Someone who only learns but never ships won't stick around

ex.healthy: You grew together, and after the split, you each took your growth with you
ex.red_flag: Only you were growing while they coasted on your results. After the split, you suddenly felt lighter
ex.test: You got better after the split = there was real nourishment. You realized you wasted your time = you were used
ex.action: Save this insight for the next one. When you meet someone similar, just ask: 'How are you going to contribute?'

personal.healthy: You mirror each other, see your own issues, and grow together
personal.red_flag: They use 'we're the same' to justify bad behavior. 'You'd do it too.' 'That's just how we are'
personal.test: Mirroring makes you reflect on yourself = healthy. Mirroring is used to justify bad behavior = red flag
personal.action: 'We're the same' doesn't mean you have to accept their bad behavior. You share the same nature, not the same choices. Behavior is a choice

seeker.healthy: The company culture fits you well, and communication is low-friction
seeker.red_flag: The company is too much like you -- the whole team thinks the same way, lacking diverse perspectives
seeker.test: The team has different types of people who complement each other = healthy. Pure echo chamber = red flag
seeker.action: Look at whether the team has people from different backgrounds. An echo chamber company will slowly calcify

company.healthy: {name} thinks in sync with the team, and communication is smooth
company.red_flag: {name} is too similar to the team -- no new perspectives coming in
company.test: Brings a different angle = healthy. Completely homogeneous = red flag
company.action: If the team is already homogeneous, {name} won't bring change. Consider hiring for complementary strengths

ex.healthy: After the split, you understand yourself better -- they were your mirror
ex.red_flag: They kept using 'we're the same' to justify bad behavior, and it took you a long time to see through it
ex.test: After the split, you can tell the difference between 'we're alike' and 'their behavior is unacceptable' = you've grown
ex.action: Someone like you doesn't mean you accept all their behavior. That ability to distinguish is what this relationship taught you

gyo.your_role: the one who carries the weight

personal.healthy: You let things unfold naturally and slowly see small positive changes
personal.red_flag: They use 'the timing isn't right' or 'let it happen naturally' to make you wait forever. Always 'give me a bit more time' with nothing changing
personal.test: Set checkpoints (three months / six months) -- small positive changes = normal. Zero improvement = it's not a timing issue
personal.action: 'I can wait, but not forever. Let's see where things are in three months' -- say it upfront

seeker.healthy: The company gives you time to grow without demanding instant results
seeker.red_flag: They say no rush but hit you with KPIs every month -- your ramp-up period becomes a performance review
seeker.test: Evaluation criteria match what they said when you joined = healthy. They said no rush but review monthly = red flag
seeker.action: Get the evaluation method in writing before joining -- in the contract or confirmed by email. Verbal promises don't count

company.healthy: {name} needs time but has real potential -- worth the wait
company.red_flag: {name} hides behind 'I need more time' and never delivers results
company.test: Small but steady progress = normal. Six months with nothing to show = red flag
company.action: Set three-month milestones. If they miss them, it's not a timing issue -- it's a capability issue

ex.healthy: This relationship taught you patience, and the process had value even if the outcome wasn't what you hoped
ex.red_flag: You kept waiting for 'things will get better when the time is right,' but they never did
ex.test: Looking back, was there any small progress at all? Yes = normal rhythm for this dynamic. No = you were strung along
ex.action: In the next relationship, say it from the start: 'I can wait, but not forever'

personal.healthy: You grow in your own incubation period, and you decide the pace of waiting
personal.red_flag: They use 'you're not ready yet' to hold you back. Whatever you want to do, they say the timing is wrong
personal.test: You feel you need to wait = healthy. They decide for you that you need to wait = red flag
personal.action: Only you can judge if you're ready. Anyone who makes that call for you is controlling you

seeker.healthy: The company has a training system that lets you grow steadily within a mature framework
seeker.red_flag: They promise training but leave you to sink or swim -- no mentor, no plan
seeker.test: Concrete training plan and mentor after joining = healthy. Left to fend for yourself = red flag
seeker.action: Ask to see the actual training plan during the interview. 'We'll arrange something' = there is no plan

company.healthy: {name} has high potential and breaks through after training
company.red_flag: {name} enjoys being trained but won't become independent -- always needs someone to hold their hand
company.test: Works independently after training = healthy. Still needs a mentor forever = red flag
company.action: Gradually reduce guidance and observe independence. If they still can't work alone after six months, they're not right for this role

ex.healthy: After the split, at some point you understood what this relationship meant -- its value plays out long-term
ex.red_flag: They kept telling you 'you're not ready yet' and used it to control what you did
ex.test: You feel free after the split = you were being controlled. You slowly came to understand = the incubation was real
ex.action: Only you can judge if you're ready. Anyone who decides that for you is controlling you
